refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path during simulated and live operational scenarios. Document network conditions and acceptance evidence.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss metrics in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of packet loss on command and feedback integrity.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, retry logic, prioritization) to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits under varying network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services shall monitor teleoperation communication quality and trigger safe-state transitions if packet loss exceeds threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure impact on packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive codec and compression algorithms that adjust payload size and encoding based on real-time network quality feedback.
DP-C: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission logic at the teleoperation protocol layer to recover lost packets without exceeding latency budgets.
DP-D: Establish network quality monitoring and telemetry reporting to measure packet loss continuously and trigger operator alerts or autonomous safe-state actions when thresholds are breached.
DP-E: Combine DP-C (FEC/retransmission) with DP-D (monitoring) and DP-B (adaptive compression) as an integrated communication resilience strategy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Implement an integrated teleoperation communication resilience architecture comprising: (1) adaptive codec/compression (DP-B) to reduce payload size and inherent loss sensitivity; (2) forward error correction and selective retransmission (DP-C) to recover lost packets within latency constraints; (3) continuous network quality monitoring and telemetry reporting (DP-D) to measure packet loss and trigger safety responses. This multi-layered approach addresses packet loss at source (compression), in-transit (FEC/retry), and detection (monitoring) levels, with explicit safety integration.

potential_sources: Coupling between adaptive compression (DP-B) and FEC overhead (DP-C): smaller payloads reduce FEC redundancy requirements, but FEC adds latency; trade-off must be tuned per network profile.
Coupling between monitoring (DP-D) and safety response: packet loss detection must trigger coordinated safe-state actions via Safety Services; requires clear interface contract.

Coupling between teleoperation module and network infrastructure: packet loss is partly a network property, not fully controllable by teleoperation logic alone; DP-E assumes network conditions are within agreed bounds.
coupling_check.notes: DP-E is not fully decoupled but represents a pragmatic, multi-layer resilience strategy. Couplings are manageable via clear interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and network SLA agreements. Recommend explicit tuning parameters (FEC ratio, compression level, retry count) to be negotiated with customer based on network profile.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and predictable (e.g., latency < 200ms, jitter < 50ms). Risk: If network conditions degrade unexpectedly, DP-E may not achieve target packet loss; mitigation: include network condition monitoring and escalation procedures.

Assumption: Latency budget for teleoperation is sufficient to accommodate FEC overhead and selective retransmission. Risk: If latency budget is tight (e.g., < 100ms round-trip), FEC/retry logic may introduce unacceptable delays; mitigation: conduct latency analysis early and define trade-off curves.

Assumption: Safety Services module can respond to packet loss alerts within safe-state transition time. Risk: If safety response is slow, operator may lose control during high packet loss events; mitigation: define maximum safe-state transition time and validate via testing.

Assumption: Adaptive compression and FEC are implementable within computational and power budgets of teleoperation hardware. Risk: If overhead is excessive, system may not meet performance or thermal constraints; mitigation: prototype and benchmark early.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-E is sound but should be refined to explicitly define: (1) packet loss measurement methodology (e.g., per-packet, per-frame, per-command); (2) FEC/retry tuning parameters and their negotiation process with customer; (3) latency budget allocation across compression, FEC, and retransmission; (4) safe-state transition criteria and timing; (5) network condition monitoring thresholds and escalation procedures. Consider adding a DP-F variant that includes network path diversity (DP-A) if customer risk profile demands ultra-high availability.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including adaptive codec/compression, forward error correction, selective retransmission, packet loss monitoring, and telemetry reporting. It interfaces with Safety Services for safe-state triggering and with the Vehicle for command execution and sensor feedback. It abstracts the underlying Network Link and presents a quality-of-service contract to operators and safety systems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits real-time packet loss metrics, network quality indicators, and communication health status to monitoring and safety systems. Includes per-frame or per-command loss rates, latency measurements, and FEC/retry statistics.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from remote operator or autonomous tasking system. Commands are encoded, compressed, and transmitted via Network Link with FEC/retry protection.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends alerts to Safety Services when packet loss exceeds threshold or network quality degrades below acceptable limits. Triggers safe-state transitions (e.g., equipment halt, return-to-home) if communication is lost.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link as the underlying transport for command and feedback transmission. Monitors link quality and adapts compression/FEC based on observed packet loss and latency.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss resilience logic. Adaptive compression, FEC, retransmission, and monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary to Safety Services for safe-state triggering. High containment score reflects strong functional cohesion and clear ownership.

Safety Services should not own packet loss resilience mechanisms (compression, FEC, retransmission). However, Safety Services must own the safe-state response logic triggered by packet loss alerts. Assigning DP-E to Safety Services would diffuse teleoperation-specific logic across module boundaries and violate separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns command execution and sensor feedback generation, not communication resilience. Assigning packet loss mitigation to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle control logic and network communication, reducing modularity and reusability. Not recommended.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could theoretically own packet loss resilience as a cross-cutting concern, doing so would bypass the Teleoperation module and create diffusion across multiple sub-systems. Better to assign to Teleoperation and have NGHE Solution orchestrate integration. Not recommended as primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-E (integrated communication resilience). It has high functional cohesion with adaptive compression, FEC, retransmission, and monitoring logic. It owns the Tasking and Telemetry interfaces and can cleanly interface with Safety Services via SafetyAlert. Diffusion is minimal: only the safe-state response logic (owned by Safety Services) crosses the boundary.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module becomes too large or complex due to FEC/compression/monitoring logic, it may violate single-responsibility principle. Mitigation: decompose DP-E into sub-modules (e.g., CompressionEngine, FECCodec, NetworkMonitor) within Teleoperation boundary, with clear internal interfaces.

Risk: If Network Link interface is not well-defined, Teleoperation may become tightly coupled to specific network technologies (e.g., 4G, 5G, satellite). Mitigation: abstract Network Link as a generic transport interface with quality-of-service parameters (latency, jitter, packet loss).

Risk: If SafetyAlert interface is not timely or reliable, safe-state transitions may be delayed or missed during high packet loss events. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a high-priority, low-latency interface with guaranteed delivery semantics.

Risk: If packet loss monitoring is not accurate or representative, acceptance criteria may not be verifiable. Mitigation: define packet loss measurement methodology (e.g., per-packet, per-frame, per-command) and validate against customer-agreed network conditions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign DP-E to Teleoperation module with the following refinements: (1) Decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (CompressionEngine, FECCodec, NetworkMonitor, TelemetryReporter) to manage complexity and maintain single-responsibility principle. (2) Define Network Link interface as a generic transport abstraction with quality-of-service parameters. (3) Define SafetyAlert interface with guaranteed delivery and low-latency semantics. (4) Establish clear ownership of packet loss measurement methodology and acceptance criteria validation. (5) Document assumptions about network conditions and escalation procedures if conditions degrade.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-E (integrated communication resilience) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1%). The multi-layer approach (adaptive compression, FEC/retransmission, monitoring) is logically sound and covers packet loss at source, in-transit, and detection levels. DP-E is implementable and testable. Coupling is manageable via clear interface contracts. Assumptions and risks are documented and mitigatable.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: SafetyAlert interface crosses Teleoperation-to-Safety Services boundary. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a well-specified, high-priority interface with clear triggering criteria and response semantics.

Diffusion risk: Network Link interface couples Teleoperation to underlying network infrastructure. Mitigation: abstract Network Link as a generic transport interface with quality-of-service parameters, decoupling from specific network technologies.

Diffusion risk: Packet loss monitoring logic may be duplicated across Teleoperation and Safety Services if not carefully scoped. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns monitoring and reporting; Safety Services owns response logic only.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment score (95) for DP-E. Functional cohesion is strong: adaptive compression, FEC, retransmission, and monitoring are all core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert crosses boundary. Ownership is clear and unambiguous. Recommend proceeding with Teleoperation as primary owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-009 is verifiable via packet loss measurement under customer-agreed network conditions. Acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss) is measurable and testable. Verification can be conducted in lab (controlled network emulation) and field (live operational scenarios). Teleoperation module provides Telemetry interface for packet loss reporting, enabling objective verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of DP-E to Teleoperation module is clear and unambiguous. Teleoperation owns adaptive compression, FEC, retransmission, and monitoring logic. Safety Services owns safe-state response logic. Vehicle owns command execution and sensor feedback. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and traceable to DP-E components.

recommended_revisions: Refine packet loss measurement methodology: define whether measurement is per-packet, per-frame, per-command, or per-session. Align with customer acceptance criteria.
Define latency budget allocation: specify maximum acceptable latency for FEC/retransmission and compression overhead. Conduct trade-off analysis to ensure latency budget is not exceeded.

Establish network condition monitoring and escalation: define thresholds for network condition degradation (e.g., latency > 200ms, jitter > 50ms) and escalation procedures if conditions exceed agreed bounds.

Document safe-state transition criteria and timing: specify maximum safe-state transition time and validate via testing. Ensure SafetyAlert interface has guaranteed delivery and low-latency semantics.
Prototype and benchmark: conduct early prototyping of adaptive compression and FEC logic to validate computational and power budgets. Identify any performance bottlenecks or thermal issues.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and has been refined into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through FR-NGHE-009.4). DP-E is sound and well-justified, but it encompasses multiple design concerns (compression, FEC, monitoring, safety integration) that warrant further decomposition. Teleoperation module is the correct owner, but its scope should be clarified via sub-module decomposition. Proceeding to the next level of functional decomposition will enable detailed design of compression algorithms, FEC codecs, monitoring logic, and safety integration, with clear ownership and interface contracts.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR-NGHE-009 and selected DP-E pass all gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM). However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through FR-NGHE-009.4) are not yet decomposed into DPs and IMs. To proceed with detailed design and implementation, each sub-FR must be mapped to a specific DP and assigned to an IM boundary. This requires a second level of functional decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss metrics in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of packet loss on command and feedback integrity.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, retry logic, prioritization) to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits under varying network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services shall monitor teleoperation communication quality and trigger safe-state transitions if packet loss exceeds threshold.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and predictable (latency < 200ms, jitter < 50ms).
Latency budget for teleoperation is sufficient to accommodate FEC overhead and selective retransmission (e.g., < 100ms round-trip).
Safety Services module can respond to packet loss alerts within safe-state transition time (e.g., < 500ms).
Adaptive compression and FEC are implementable within computational and power budgets of teleoperation hardware.
Packet loss measurement methodology is agreed with customer and is representative of operational scenarios.
Network Link interface provides quality-of-service metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) to Teleoperation module.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade unexpectedly, causing DP-E to fail to achieve target packet loss. Mitigation: include network condition monitoring and escalation procedures.
Latency budget may be insufficient for FEC/retry logic, introducing unacceptable delays. Mitigation: conduct latency analysis early and define trade-off curves.
Safe-state transition may be slow, causing operator to lose control during high packet loss events. Mitigation: define maximum safe-state transition time and validate via testing.
Adaptive compression and FEC overhead may exceed computational or power budgets. Mitigation: prototype and benchmark early.

Packet loss measurement may not be accurate or representative, making acceptance criteria unverifiable. Mitigation: define measurement methodology and validate against customer-agreed network conditions.
evidence_needed: Packet loss measurement data under customer-agreed network conditions (lab and field).
Latency analysis and trade-off curves for FEC/compression overhead.
Computational and power budget analysis for adaptive compression and FEC.
Network condition monitoring and escalation procedure documentation.
Prototype and benchmark results for compression algorithms and FEC codecs.
SafetyAlert interface specification and guaranteed delivery validation.
Network Link interface specification and quality-of-service parameter definition.
